## [00:00 - 03:01]

What I have learned in my practice is that a lot of people don't really understand how businesses work. So we're going to walk through kind of a roadmap of how businesses work and the different types of businesses for you. And we're going to dissect it a little bit. But there are some big picture reasons of why you want to think about having a business formation instead of just going out there and doing business. First and foremost is creating credibility. I don't know if you think about it, but I do. If you're out there and somebody hands you a business card and it's just their name on it, you're okay. Maybe you want to do business with them or buy whatever products you're sure to say have. But when you get a business card and it has ink or LLC behind it, it adds an additional little bit of weight. And we're going to talk about why that is. If you look like a business, you act like a business, then people are going to treat you like a business. It's going to be easier for you to go out there and get lines of credit for things. It's going to be easier for you to get payment processing, meaning the ability to accept credit cards for your business. It's going to be easier for you out there and just create credibility when you meet people because having a formalized business entity, it creates this mystique, this allure of like, Hey, I'm something. I'm different. So what we're really trying to talk about is the difference of just going out there and doing business by yourself and creating an alter and other entity, a structure that's separate and distinct from you. So if you go to the internal revenue service, the IRS, they keep statistics on the most frequently used type of businesses that are out there. ## [03:01 - 06:04]

But what if your partner goes out and engages in some sort of transaction that you're not there and you're not aware of, but it looks like he has the authority to enter it for you as a partner and then he decides to renege on that. He breaches his contract with somebody and they end up suing. Well, guess what? As a partner, you're not just liable for yourself, you're liable for whatever your partner does. Well, that sounds worse. And it is worse a lot of times. So let's talk sole practitioner first and foremost. Well, guess who's liable for that? It's just you saying, hey, I'm out there selling this business. I'm selling my knowledge to third parties who are buying this. ## [06:04 - 09:06]

organizations. The first and foremost is called the corporation. Now corporations have been in existence for a long time. I think the origins of corporations go clear back to the Romans. They decided, let's have a separate and distinct entity. It's not me personally. I can go out and I can do business, but any liability that pertains to that business transaction stays with that business. And if we do it there, that means, hey, it stays there. It doesn't leak into my personal life. Now corporations are awesome because they provide that shield of liability, but they have some limitations as well. First and foremost, what I found when individuals set up corporations, they generally don't go through the formalities required to maintain the corporation. Well, what's that Greg? What are formalities? Well, one of the formalities you have when you set up a corporation is you have to have things like annual shareholders meetings. So I found out early in my career when I set up corporations for clients that guess what they weren't doing? They weren't doing annual shareholders meetings. They weren't sending out notice. They weren't keeping minutes. It was like, oh my gosh, you're not following the corporate formalities. And if you don't follow the corporate formalities, that allows your creditor that's out here that says, oh, this is a distinct and separate entity than you, it allows them to come through and say, no, they're not following the right procedure. So I'm going to go and bypass that corporation and sue them individually as a shareholder. The owners of corporations, they're called shareholders. So you as the owner of the corporation, they could go after you and sue you. And if they show that you didn't follow the corporate formalities, then you could be personally liable for the activities at corporation. Well, that's hard. Well, most individuals, when they go out and they do business, they don't really follow that. Second major hindrance to corporations is they're out there. All the big Fortune 500 companies are generally C corporations. Why? Because you get the maximum amount of deductions for those corporations when they're a C corporation. There are more write offs and expenses you can take than you can in other business ventures. But the problem is, if you're like me and you're starting small, you're taking your knowledge, you're creating a business, maybe you don't have a lot of employees, maybe you're the only employee, well, what's the benefit for you doing that as a C corporation? Not getting all the deductions you can take under the internal revenue code, you're just out there and you're okay, you're operating. And then you're stuck with a really dirty situation called double taxation. Double taxation is the concept that a C corporation is taxed at two levels. One, it's taxed at a level when you're a corporation, so it gets taxed at corporate tax. And it has to pay that. Then it takes the money that you just pay tax on and it distributes it to shareholders, which is you, and then you pay taxes again. So you pay taxes twice. Once at a corporate level, once at an individual level. Well, who wants to do that? Well, if you don't follow the corporate formalities and you don't know how to take deductions from a corporation, it opens a lot of liabilities. So in the 1970s, state legislatures across the United States decided, well, hey, what if you had the benefit of a corporation to have protections, but you also have the benefit

## [09:06 - 12:10]

of a partnership because partnerships only pay taxes on the proceeds that they individually receive. So if I'm a partner and my partner makes 60, take $60,000 and I only take $40,000, I only pay taxes on 40, not on 60. Well that's a pretty good deal, right? Okay. Why don't we combine that partnership taxation with this protection and get rid of some of the corporate formalities and create something that people can actually use? Well, they called it a limited liability company. So an LLC is a state-run organization. It's every different state has different LLC acts and laws that pertain to that. Now there are four states that I predominantly go to that have the best laws as it pertains to LLCs. That would be Wyoming, Nevada, Delaware, and Alaska. Now generally when you're doing business, so I would use an LLC setup in the state where you predominantly preside or you do business. It's easier that way, but sometimes we may not be in the country, for example, and want to take advantage of some state law that allows us to sell in the United States, but not be a US citizen. Well, we can do an LLC in one of those four states, or maybe I'm in a state that doesn't really have good asset protection laws and has expensive fees and I want to do it in Wyoming or in one of the other states that I just mentioned. Well you can do that with an LLC. An LLC has great amount of flexibility. You don't have to follow the corporate formalities of a corporation. There is not double taxation. There's a way to avoid double taxation with LLCs. And you get this protection so that if you're out there doing business, it still stays tangibly separate and distinct.
